data.title: LLY joins $1T club but doesn't feature in SA's top 10 large-cap healthcare stocks
data.description:
Eli Lilly (LLY) officially joined the elite group of U.S. companies valued at more than $1T on Friday, becoming the tenth American stock to currently cross the landmark threshold.

The move came as shares advanced 1%, driven by persistent investor enthusiasm for the pharmaceutical giant’s fast-growing GLP-1 drug franchise, which continues to reshape expectations across the broader health care sector. 

Despite achieving the trillion-dollar milestone, Lilly (LLY) does not currently rank among Seeking Alpha’s top ten healthcare stocks based on its Quant Ratings framework. 

According to the SA’s proprietary scoring system, LLY holds a Quant Rating of 3.47, placing it outside the upper tier. Below is a breakdown of the highest-rated healthcare names as measured by Seeking Alpha’s Quant Grades (note that these are only companies with a market cap of $10B and above): 

No. 1: Guardant Health (GH), Quant Rating of 4.95.

No. 2: Incyte Corporation (INCY), Quant Rating of 4.93.

No. 3: Pfizer (PFE), Quant Rating of 4.91.

No. 4: Merck & Co. (MRK), Quant Rating of 4.87.

No. 5: Roivant Sciences Ltd. (ROIV), Quant Rating of 4.82.

No. 6: Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), Quant Rating of 4.70.

No. 7: Astellas Pharma Inc. (OTCPK:ALPMY), Quant Rating of 4.69.

No. 8: Illumina (ILMN), Quant Rating of 4.62.

No. 9: Neurocrine Biosciences (NBIX), Quant Rating of 4.58.

No. 10: Exelixis, Inc. (EXEL), Quant Rating of 4.58.

For more on the healthcare space, investors can look to gain diversified exposure to the space through exchange-traded funds. 

Health Care ETFs: (XLV), (VHT), (IHI), (IXJ), (IYH), (FHLC), (FXH), (IBB), (XBI), (FBT), (BBH), (PBE), (IDNA), (ARKG), (GNOM), (BIB), and (HELX).

data.title: Doximity upgraded at Raymond James on improving long-term growth visibility
data.description:
• Raymond James upgraded Doximity (DOCS) to strong buy from outperform, saying that the digital platform for doctors with a "current risk/reward at 25x [free cash flow] looking too compelling to ignore."
  • The firm, however, lowered its target price to $65 from $75 (40% upside based on Nov. 20 close).
  • Analyst Brian Peterson notes that while some investors have concerns about seasonality and guidance philosophy, the company has also been ramping up workflow adoption, multi-product expansion, and client portal benefits.
  • Peterson said that Doximity has seen annual growth rates from 2023 to 2025 of 15%-18%, with quarterly growth rates in that period experiencing peaks in the mid-20% range and troughing in the mid-single digit range. This, he added, is a sign that "seasonal shifts are not indicative of longer-term growth rates."
  • "...we're comfortable underwriting a double-digit growth trajectory for the foreseeable future," Peterson wrote.

data.title: Strategy's financing model tested as preferreds slide with bitcoin - report
data.description:
Strategy's (MSTR) preferred shares fell alongside bitcoin (BTC-USD) this week, pushing up implied yields and likely making future funding for BTC purchases more expensive, according to a media report on Friday.

The crypto treasury giant has aggressively tapped debt and equity markets this year to load up on bitcoin (BTC-USD). It has issued billions of dollars in high-yield preferred stock, such as (STRK), (STRF) (STRD) and (STRC), alongside its convertible debt.

But, with bitcoin (BTC-USD) under pressure, a $1.2B, 10% Strategy (MSTR) preferred offering that was priced in June at 85 cents on the dollar has dropped to 66 cents, lifting its yield to about 15%, according to data compiled by Bloomberg, which added that prices on other issues have also fallen.

Beyond raising questions about Strategy's (MSTR) ability to keep raising money for new bitcoin (BTC-USD) buys, the slide in preferreds also clouds its capacity to meet dividend obligations on existing preferred stock. Because those payouts are paid in dollars and euros, the company must continue issuing new securities to generate the cash. The dividends are not payable in BTC.

So far this year, Michael Saylor's Strategy (MSTR) has sold five preferred stock series, Bloomberg reported, noting bankers usually look to the yields on outstanding preferreds to determine pricing for new offerings. 

Bloomberg pointed out that Strategy (MSTR) is not required to make payments on its existing preferreds and can skip dividend payouts. But such moves risk souring demand for subsequent offerings, and some preferreds grant holders a board seat if the company does not pay dividends.

Strategy (MSTR) did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Earlier on Friday, JPMorgan warned that Strategy (MSTR) is at risk of being removed from MSCI's equity indices, a move that could trigger billions of dollars in outflows.

MSTR common stock is on track to close out the week ended Nov. 21 with a 16% drop, coinciding with bitcoin's 12% slump.

data.title: AI companies' use of leverage bears close watching, Fed's Jefferson says
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Philip Jefferson sees important differences between the dot-com boom and bust of the late 1990s and the current surge of valuations in artificial intelligence companies, he said Friday. 

That's not to say there are no concerns about AI's ability to threaten financial stability. For example, in a Fed survey, 30% of contracts considered a turn in the sentiment toward AI as a salient risk to the U.S. financial system and the global economy, up from 9% in a spring survey. "Participants noted that if such an unwinding were to occur, it could tighten financial conditions and restrain economic activity more broadly," Jefferson pointed out. He's not making a judgment on the likelihood of the scenario, but highlights the importance of monitoring such scenarios.

He observed several clear differences between the current AI boom and the dot-com era:

  • Many publicly traded dot-com firms had little or no realized earnings and sometimes only speculative revenue compared with today's AI-focused stocks, which "generally have well-established and growing earnings streams."
  • Price-earnings ratios of AI-related firms have remained well below peak ratios of dot-com companies in the late1990s-early 2000s.
  • "The proliferation of dot-com firms in the late 1990s and early 2000s suggests a less discriminating enthusiasm than what we are observing in today's market," Jefferson said. At its peak in the late 1990s, more than 1,000 firms were publicly listed as dot-come companies. By one measure, only about 50 publicly traded firms are considered to be AI-focused enterprises, he said.

One common factor is that both dot-com and AI firms until recently demonstrated little reliance on debt financing, which may reduce the impact from a shift in sentiment toward AI through credit markets. However, recent reports suggest that AI companies may be increasing their use of debt through public and private credit markets to fund the substantial investments in computing infrastructure and talent acquisition, Jefferson said.

"If that turns out to be the case, leverage in the AI sector could increase—and so could the losses if sentiment toward AI shifts. I will watch this developing trend closely," Jefferson said.

He also emphasized that the developments in AI are taking place against a "backdrop of a financial system that is sound and resilient."

Related ETFs:  iShares Future AI & Tech ETF (ARTY); Amplify AI Powered Equity ETF (AIEQ), and REX AI Equity Premium Income ETF (AIPI).

data.title: Best Quant-rated small- and mid-cap healthcare stocks as LLY hits $1T in market cap
data.description:
As Eli Lilly (LLY) becomes the first pharmaceutical company to hit $1T in market cap, Seeking Alpha takes a look at some of the best Quant-rated mid-to-small-cap healthcare stocks.

In today’s volatile market, investors are constantly searching for stocks with strong growth potential and solid financial fundamentals. These companies are still under $7B in market cap but have been rated “Strong Buy” by Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating system.

The stocks featured in this list have been filtered through Seeking Alpha’s proprietary Quant Model. This sophisticated trading algorithm evaluates stocks based on multiple factors, including growth, profitability, value, momentum, and rising analysts’ earnings estimates.

The model has proven remarkably effective, with Seeking Alpha’s very bullish Quant Stock recommendations outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500) by 1,755% versus 379% from January 2010 to June 2021.

Top small-cap and mid-cap healthcare stocks by Quant:

  1. Inhibrx Biosciences (INBX) - Strong Buy rating (4.99), up 108.90% year-to-date, $1.16B market cap
  2. Nektar Therapeutics (NKTR) - Strong Buy rating (4.98), up 434.71% year-to-date, $1.12B market cap
  3. GRAIL, Inc. (GRAL) - Strong Buy rating (4.97), up 117.83% year-to-date, $3.34B market cap

data.title: AI angst: The Nvidia shadow hangs over Tesla and other EV stocks
data.description:
Electric vehicle stocks traded lower on Friday as the sector reacted to the general market downturn and sudden shift to risk-off trading.

Also in the mix, short-term headwinds for the EV sector include pricing pressures, mixed signals on consumer demand, and regulatory transitions. EV names are also high-beta stocks in general, meaning that they are generally more volatile directionally with broad market moves.  

In morning trading, VinFast Auto (VFS) was down 11.8% after reporting Q3 earnings. Other decliners included Aptera Motors (SEV) off 6.0%, Rivian Automotive (RIVN) shedding 4.0%, Tesla (TSLA) declining 2.4%, and Lucid Group (LCID) dropping 2.0%.

Notably, over the last six weeks, General Motors (GM) and Ford Motor (F) have outperformed the broad auto sector, due in part to reeling in some of their more ambitious EV plans. As for Tesla (TSLA), its share price has its highest correlation in its trading history to Nvidia (NVDA) and other tech names at the forefront of the AI trade. The trading correlation between Nvidia (NVDA) and Tesla (TSLA) is now one of the highest of any S&P 500 Index component pairs.

data.title: Crypto will be the canary in the coal mine for the Fed riding to the rescue
data.description:
The cryptocurrency markets (BTC-USD), (ETH-USD) will be the first to detect when the Federal Reserve might intervene with interest rate cuts, according to Michael Hartnett, chief investment strategist at BofA.

Crypto recently experienced its second-largest outflow on record with $2.2B in outflows in a single week, indicating significant investor concern despite the overall $52B of inflows recorded so far in 2025.

 

 

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has dropped 30% and Ethereum (ETH-USD) has plummeted 41% from peak to trough. In addition, institutional investors have allocated only 0.4% of their portfolios to crypto assets, according to BofA’s November Global Fund Manager Survey, while retail investors poured a record $46 billion into the sector in 2025.

 

 

 

Derivatives now represent 74% of all cryptocurrency trading volume, reflecting what Hartnett described as “peak liquidity” conditions driven by “bubbly animal spirits.”

These market dynamics are reportedly fueled by widespread rate cuts over the past two years and expectations for additional cuts in 2026, creating a scenario where cryptocurrency movements may signal broader market shifts requiring Federal Reserve action.

Hartnett suggested that as in December, cryptocurrency volatility could represent “the quickest route to Fed capitulation” on interest rates.

Odds of a 25-basis point interest rate cut by the Fed have jumped to 73.3%, according to the CME FedWatch Tool.

Bitcoin Backed ETFs: (IBIT), (ARKB), (GBTC), (BRRR), (BTCO), (HODL), (BTCW), (FBTC), (BITB), and (EZBC).
